The number of partitions per topic determines the maximum parallelism for consumers. Each record in a partition has a monotonically increasing Offset — the record's position in the partition. Offsets are per-partition, so offset 5 in partition 0 is different from offset 5 in partition 1. Producers write records to topics, choosing which partition using a partitioning strategy: round-robin for even distribution, key-based hashing so all records with the same key go to the same partition (guaranteeing order for that key). Consumers read records from partitions by tracking their current offset. A Consumer Group is a set of consumers that together consume a topic — each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer within the group, enabling parallel consumption. If you have 6 partitions and 3 consumers in a group, each consumer handles 2 partitions. Adding more consumers than partitions means some consumers are idle. Kafka brokers are the servers storing partitions. A Kafka cluster has multiple brokers for fault tolerance — each partition is replicated across multiple brokers with one broker acting as the partition leader (handling reads and writes) and others as followers.
hint: Topic = category. Partition = ordered log (unit of parallelism). Offset = position in partition. Consumer group = each partition assigned to one consumer.

question: What are Kafka's three message delivery guarantees — At-Most-Once, At-Least-Once, and Exactly-Once? How does each work and what are the tradeoffs?
answer: Kafka provides three delivery semantics that represent a fundamental tradeoff between performance, complexity, and correctness. At-Most-Once means a message is delivered zero or one time — the producer sends and forgets, and the consumer commits its offset before processing. If the consumer crashes after committing but before processing, the message is lost forever. If the producer sends and the broker doesn't acknowledge, the producer doesn't retry, so the message may be lost. Best for: fire-and-forget analytics, non-critical logging where losing occasional events is acceptable. Performance is highest because no retry logic is needed. At-Least-Once means every message is delivered one or more times — the producer retries on failure (may send the same message multiple times if the acknowledgment is lost), and the consumer only commits its offset after successfully processing. If the consumer crashes after processing but before committing, it will reprocess on restart. Best for: most use cases where occasional duplicate processing is acceptable and consumers are idempotent. This is the default and most common setting. Exactly-Once means every message is delivered and processed precisely once — the hardest guarantee to achieve. Kafka implements this through idempotent producers (each message has a sequence number; broker deduplicates retries), and transactional APIs (atomic read-process-write across topics). The consumer reads, processes, and commits offset in a single atomic Kafka transaction. Best for: financial transactions, inventory updates where duplicates cause real problems. Highest complexity and overhead.
hint: At-most-once = fast, can lose. At-least-once = duplicates possible, consumer must be idempotent. Exactly-once = atomic transactions, highest overhead.

question: What happens when a Kafka consumer fails — how does offset management and consumer group rebalancing work? What is the 'at least once' pitfall with auto-commit?
answer: When a Kafka consumer fails or a new consumer joins a consumer group, Kafka triggers a rebalancing — the process of reassigning partitions across the available consumers. The Group Coordinator (a designated Kafka broker) detects the change (via heartbeat timeout for failures, or a JoinGroup request for new members). All consumers in the group stop consuming and rejoin the group. The Group Leader (the first consumer to join) receives the full member list and determines the partition assignment using a partition assignment strategy (RangeAssignor, RoundRobinAssignor, StickyAssignor). The StickyAssignor minimizes partition movement — it keeps existing assignments where possible, reducing the 'stop the world' effect of full rebalancing. Offset management is critical for correct recovery. Kafka stores consumer offsets in an internal topic `__consumer_offsets`. When a consumer crashes and restarts (or another consumer takes over its partitions), it reads the last committed offset for each partition and resumes from there. The danger with auto-commit: Kafka's default is enable.auto.commit=true with auto.commit.interval.ms=5000. This commits the offset of the last polled batch every 5 seconds automatically. If the consumer processes messages from a batch and crashes before the 5-second auto-commit fires, on restart it re-reads and reprocesses those same messages. This is the at-least-once pitfall — you must either handle duplicates in your processing logic, or use manual offset commits after successful processing.
hint: Rebalance = partition reassignment when consumers join/leave. Auto-commit pitfall = crash between process and commit = duplicate on restart. Use manual commit + idempotent processing.

question: How does Kafka achieve high throughput — what are the storage and I/O optimizations that make Kafka faster than traditional message brokers?
answer: Kafka can sustain millions of messages per second because of several key design decisions that align with how modern hardware works. Sequential disk writes: Kafka appends records to partition log files sequentially. Sequential disk writes on modern SSDs and HDDs are 100x-1000x faster than random writes because the disk head doesn't need to seek. Traditional databases with random B-tree writes are much slower. Kafka's append-only log structure means it never modifies existing data — only appends. Zero-copy data transfer: when a consumer reads from Kafka, the traditional path is: disk → kernel buffer → user space → kernel socket buffer → network. This requires two copies and two context switches. Kafka uses the Linux sendfile() system call which transfers data directly from the disk file to the network socket via the kernel, bypassing user space entirely. This zero-copy transfer eliminates two data copies and dramatically reduces CPU usage. Batching: Kafka batches multiple records together before writing to disk and before sending to consumers, amortizing the overhead of each I/O operation across many records. Network latency is a big cost — sending 1000 records in one network call is far cheaper than 1000 individual calls. Page cache: Kafka relies heavily on the OS page cache rather than managing its own memory cache. Reading from page cache is as fast as reading from RAM. Since Kafka's reads are typically sequential from recent data, the page cache hit rate is high. This also means Kafka doesn't compete with the JVM heap for memory — more OS memory goes to page cache.
hint: Kafka is fast because: sequential disk writes (no seeking), zero-copy sendfile(), batching (fewer network calls), page cache (OS caches hot data in RAM).

question: When should you use Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs a database queue? What are the key decision signals for each?
answer: The choice between Kafka, RabbitMQ, and a database queue depends on your requirements around throughput, message retention, consumer model, and operational complexity. Use a Database Queue (polling a DB table) when the message volume is low (< 1000 messages/minute), you already have a database and don't want to add infrastructure, messages need to be tightly coupled to database transactions (write order and enqueue atomically), and operational simplicity matters more than performance. The downside is that polling adds load to the database. Use RabbitMQ when you need sophisticated routing (messages routed to different queues based on content/type via Exchange rules), you want push-based delivery to consumers (Kafka is pull-based), messages should be deleted after consumption (not retained), you have complex retry and dead-letter queue requirements with fine-grained control, and message throughput is moderate (up to ~50K messages/sec). RabbitMQ is a traditional message broker designed for reliable task distribution. Use Kafka when you need very high throughput (millions of messages/second), message retention is important (consumers can replay historical events), multiple independent consumer groups each need to process all messages, you need ordered processing within a partition (guaranteed order by key), and you are building event sourcing, audit logs, or real-time stream processing pipelines. Kafka is an event log, not a traditional queue — messages are not deleted on consumption.
hint: DB queue = simple, transactional, low volume. RabbitMQ = routing + push + task distribution. Kafka = high throughput + replay + multiple independent consumers.

question: What is Elasticsearch and how does it differ from a relational database? Explain its core concepts: indices, documents, shards, and how it enables full-text search.
answer: Elasticsearch is a distributed search and analytics engine built on top of Apache Lucene. It differs from a relational database in its fundamental purpose: relational databases are optimized for exact lookups and structured queries (give me all orders where user_id=123), while Elasticsearch is optimized for full-text search, fuzzy matching, and relevance ranking (give me the most relevant products matching 'wireless bluetooth headphones'). Core concepts: an Index in Elasticsearch is analogous to a database table — it is a collection of documents with similar characteristics. A Document is the basic unit of data, stored as JSON. Each document has fields (like columns in SQL but schema can be flexible). A Shard is a fully functional independent Lucene index. Each Elasticsearch index is split into primary shards for horizontal scaling — documents are distributed across shards using a routing mechanism (default: hash of document ID). Replicas are copies of primary shards for fault tolerance and read scaling. Full-text search works through an inverted index maintained per shard. When a document is indexed, its text fields are analyzed — tokenized into terms, lowercased, stemmed (run/running/ran all become 'run'), and stopwords removed. These terms are stored in the inverted index with their frequencies and positions. At search time, the query text goes through the same analysis pipeline, terms are looked up in the inverted index, matching documents are scored by relevance (BM25 by default), and results from all shards are merged and ranked.
hint: Elasticsearch = distributed Lucene for full-text search. Text analyzed into terms → inverted index. Shards for scale. BM25 for relevance scoring. Combines structured + text search.

question: How do you sync Elasticsearch with your primary database? Explain the dual-write, CDC, and event-driven sync patterns and their tradeoffs.
answer: Elasticsearch is a search index, not a primary data store — your source of truth lives in a relational database (PostgreSQL, MySQL) or another durable store. Keeping Elasticsearch in sync with the primary database is a critical design problem. Dual-Write: the application writes to both the primary database and Elasticsearch in the same request. Simple to implement but has a critical failure mode: if the DB write succeeds but the ES write fails (network issue, ES is down), data is inconsistent. If the ES write succeeds but DB write fails and you rollback, ES has stale data. Dual-write also doesn't handle bulk historical backfills well. Change Data Capture (CDC): a CDC tool like Debezium monitors the database's transaction log (MySQL binlog, PostgreSQL WAL) and streams every change event (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) to Kafka. An Elasticsearch sync service consumes from Kafka and applies changes to the index. This is robust because the transaction log is the source of truth — if Kafka or ES is temporarily down, CDC catches up from where it left off. The database log is the single source of truth and ES is an eventually consistent derived view. Event-Driven Sync: when business events happen, they are published to Kafka (e.g., 'product updated'). An indexing service consumes events and updates ES. This is cleaner for microservices but requires all write paths to publish events consistently. The CDC approach is generally preferred for its reliability and ability to handle any data change without modifying application code.
hint: CDC with Debezium (DB WAL → Kafka → ES) is most reliable. Dual-write is simple but inconsistency risk. CDC handles any DB change even without app code changes.

question: Design a search typeahead (autocomplete) system like Google's search suggestions. What data structures and caching strategies enable sub-100ms suggestions?
answer: A typeahead system shows search suggestions as the user types, with each keystroke triggering a lookup. The challenge is sub-100ms response time for millions of concurrent users. The Trie (prefix tree) data structure is the foundation of typeahead: each node represents a character, and a path from root to a node represents a prefix. Each terminal node stores the completion and its popularity score. A search for 'sys' traverses three nodes and returns all completions under that node sorted by score. A trie with millions of entries is too large for a single machine — it is sharded by prefix. 'a-f' prefixes go to shard 1, 'g-m' to shard 2, etc. For production, the trie is pre-computed offline from search logs (which searches are most popular?) and loaded into memory for fast lookup. The trie is rebuilt periodically (hourly or daily) as search trends change. Caching is critical since the same prefixes are queried by millions of users. Results for prefixes like 'se', 'sys', 'syst' are cached in Redis with high TTL. Cache hit rate for common prefixes is near 100%. For freshness, a Kafka pipeline processes real-time searches and updates a trending prefix store — recently trending searches (like a breaking news event) can surface in suggestions within minutes. The client also debounces requests — instead of firing on every keystroke, it waits 100-200ms of inactivity, dramatically reducing backend load.
hint: Trie = prefix tree for O(prefix_length) lookups. Pre-compute from search logs. Cache prefix results in Redis. Client debounce reduces calls. Shard trie by prefix range.

question: What is the difference between a Forward Proxy and a Reverse Proxy? What does Nginx do and what are the main use cases for each type of proxy?
answer: A Forward Proxy sits between clients and the internet, acting on behalf of clients. Clients know they are using a proxy and direct their requests to it. The proxy forwards requests to the internet and returns responses. Use cases: corporate network filtering (block access to certain sites), privacy/anonymization (hide client's real IP address from destination servers), caching for a group of users (proxy caches responses so multiple users benefit), and bypassing geo-restrictions. The destination server sees the proxy's IP, not the client's. A VPN is a form of forward proxy. A Reverse Proxy sits between the internet and backend servers, acting on behalf of servers. Clients do NOT know about the backend servers — they think they're talking directly to the service. The reverse proxy receives requests and forwards them to one of many backend servers. Use cases: load balancing (distribute traffic across server pool), SSL termination (handle HTTPS at the proxy, communicate with backends over HTTP), caching (serve cached responses without hitting backend), compression (compress responses before sending to clients), DDoS protection (absorb and filter attack traffic before it reaches backends), and security (hide backend server IPs and architecture). Nginx is the most popular reverse proxy and web server. It handles millions of concurrent connections efficiently using an event-driven, non-blocking architecture — a single Nginx process handles thousands of connections using epoll, unlike Apache which creates a new thread per connection.
hint: Forward proxy = acts for clients (VPN, content filtering). Reverse proxy = acts for servers (load balancing, SSL, caching). Nginx = event-driven, handles 10K+ connections per process.

question: What is service discovery in microservices and why is it needed? Explain client-side vs server-side discovery and how tools like Consul or Kubernetes enable it.
answer: In a microservices architecture, services run on dynamic infrastructure where IP addresses and ports change constantly — containers restart, scale up/down, and migrate across hosts. Service discovery solves the problem of services finding each other without hardcoding IP addresses. Without service discovery, you would hardcode service addresses which breaks the moment the service moves to a different container or server. There are two patterns: Client-Side Discovery means the client is responsible for determining the location of a service. The client queries a service registry (like Consul or Etcd) to get the list of available instances of the target service, then uses a load balancing algorithm to pick one and calls it directly. The client has full control over load balancing but must implement this logic in every client and in every language your services use. Server-Side Discovery means the client sends requests to a load balancer or router (like Kubernetes Service or an API Gateway), which queries the service registry and routes the request to an available instance. The client has no knowledge of the service registry — it just calls a stable address. This is simpler for clients but adds a hop. Kubernetes implements server-side discovery natively: each Service gets a stable DNS name and virtual IP. Kubernetes' kube-proxy routes requests to healthy Pod IPs. When Pods scale up or down, kube-proxy automatically updates routing tables. The service's DNS name never changes, only the backing Pod IPs change behind the scenes.
hint: Client-side discovery = client queries registry then calls directly. Server-side = call stable address, router handles registry lookup. Kubernetes services provide server-side discovery natively.

question: How does the Circuit Breaker pattern work? Explain the three states (Closed, Open, Half-Open) and how it prevents cascading failures in microservices.
answer: The Circuit Breaker pattern is named after electrical circuit breakers — when a downstream service is failing, the circuit breaker 'trips' and stops sending requests to that service, preventing a slow or failing dependency from cascading into a full system failure. Without a circuit breaker: Service A calls Service B which is down. Service A's threads block waiting for timeout (say 30 seconds). With 1000 concurrent requests to Service A, all 1000 threads are blocked waiting for Service B. Service A's thread pool is exhausted. Service A is now also down — the failure cascaded. The three states: Closed state is normal operation — all requests pass through to the downstream service. The circuit breaker monitors the failure rate. If failures exceed a threshold (e.g., 50% failure rate in the last 10 seconds), the breaker trips to Open. Open state — all requests fail immediately without calling the downstream service. A fallback response is returned instantly (cached data, default response, or error message). This fails fast rather than blocking threads. The circuit stays open for a configurable wait time (e.g., 60 seconds) to allow the downstream service to recover. After the wait time, the circuit transitions to Half-Open. Half-Open state — a limited number of test requests are allowed through to the downstream service. If they succeed, the circuit closes (service recovered). If they fail, the circuit opens again and the wait timer resets. This self-healing mechanism automatically recovers when the downstream service is healthy again.
hint: Closed = normal. Open = fail fast (don't call failing service). Half-Open = test if recovered. Prevents thread pool exhaustion from slow downstream services.

question: What is the Saga Pattern for distributed transactions? Compare Choreography-based vs Orchestration-based Sagas and explain compensating transactions.
answer: In microservices, each service has its own database — you cannot have a traditional database transaction spanning multiple services. The Saga Pattern manages a sequence of local transactions across multiple services, ensuring data consistency without distributed locks. Each step is a local transaction in one service's database. If a step fails, compensating transactions undo the previous steps. A Saga is a sequence of (transaction, compensating transaction) pairs. Two implementation styles: Choreography-based Saga: services react to events without a central coordinator. Each service publishes events after completing its local transaction. Other services listen to these events and trigger their own transactions. Simple to implement with Kafka. However, it is hard to track the overall saga state and debug failures — the flow is implicit across multiple event handlers. For complex sagas with many steps, the event chains become hard to understand. Orchestration-based Saga: a central Saga Orchestrator manages the entire flow. It explicitly calls each service in sequence and handles failures by calling compensating transactions. The flow is explicit and visible in one place. Easier to add new steps or modify the flow. The tradeoff is that the orchestrator becomes a central point of coupling. Compensating transactions must be idempotent (safe to call multiple times if the compensation itself fails and is retried). Order example: create order (local tx), reserve inventory (local tx), process payment (local tx), notify user (local tx). If payment fails: compensating tx releases inventory reservation and cancels the order.
hint: Saga = sequence of local transactions with compensating transactions on failure. Choreography = events (decentralized). Orchestration = coordinator (explicit flow). Compensations must be idempotent.

question: Work through a complete back-of-envelope estimation for designing Twitter: How many servers, how much storage, and what read/write QPS do you need for 300M DAU?
answer: This is a complete worked example of back-of-envelope estimation for a Twitter-scale system. Step 1 — Define the system: 300M DAU. Average user checks timeline 5 times per day. Average user sends 1 tweet every 3 days. Average tweet size: 200 bytes text + 0.3 tweets have media (average media size 200KB). Step 2 — Write QPS: 300M users / 3 days = 100M tweets/day. 100M / 86,400 seconds ≈ 1,157 tweet writes/second. Peak is ~3x average: 3,500 writes/second. Step 3 — Read QPS: 300M users × 5 timeline views/day = 1.5B timeline reads/day. 1.5B / 86,400 ≈ 17,361 reads/second. Peak: ~52,000 reads/second. Read:Write ratio ≈ 15:1 (read heavy — cache aggressively). Step 4 — Storage: 100M tweets/day × 200 bytes = 20GB/day for text. Media: 100M × 0.3 × 200KB = 6TB/day for media. Over 5 years: text = 20GB × 365 × 5 ≈ 36TB. Media = 6TB × 365 × 5 ≈ 10PB. Step 5 — Servers: Peak read QPS = 52,000. Each app server handles ~1,000 QPS. Need ~52 app servers (with buffer: ~100). Cache servers: 20% of content handles 80% of traffic. 100M tweets/day × 200B × 20% active = 4GB active tweet cache. 2-3 Redis servers handles this. Step 6 — Bandwidth: Peak read bandwidth = 52,000 QPS × 200 bytes = 10MB/sec (tweet text only, media served via CDN).
hint: Always: DAU → daily requests → avg QPS → peak QPS (3x avg) → storage per day → 5yr storage → servers needed (peak QPS / server capacity). Read:Write ratio drives caching strategy.

question: Work through a complete back-of-envelope estimation for WhatsApp: QPS, storage, and connection count for 2 billion users sending 100 billion messages per day.
answer: WhatsApp scale estimation — a complete worked example. Given: 2B registered users, 500M DAU, 100B messages sent per day. Average message size: 100 bytes for text, 20% of messages have media (average 100KB). Step 1 — Message QPS: 100B messages / 86,400 seconds ≈ 1.16M messages/second. Peak: ~3.5M messages/second. This is write QPS to the message store. Step 2 — Delivery QPS: each message must be delivered to recipient(s). For 1:1 chats (majority), delivery QPS ≈ message QPS. For group chats (average 10 members), delivery QPS = message QPS × 10 = 35M deliveries/second at peak. Step 3 — Active WebSocket connections: 500M DAU, assume 40% online at peak = 200M concurrent WebSocket connections. Each connection needs a server thread or event handler. An optimized WebSocket server handles 100K connections per server. Servers for connections: 200M / 100K = 2,000 connection servers. Step 4 — Storage: text messages: 100B/day × 80% text × 100 bytes = 8TB/day. Media: 100B × 20% × 100KB = 2PB/day. With 7-day retention: 2PB × 7 = 14PB of media. For permanent storage, WhatsApp uses end-to-end encrypted blob storage. Step 5 — Key insight: the enormous scale (1.16M msg/sec) requires Kafka or equivalent to handle the write ingestion, with the message store being a NoSQL database like Cassandra optimized for high write throughput.
hint: 100B messages/day = 1.16M QPS. Group delivery multiplies by group size. 200M concurrent WebSockets = 2000 servers. Media at 2PB/day is the biggest cost driver.

question: Work through a complete back-of-envelope estimation for a Google Docs-like collaborative document editing system for 50M DAU. How many servers, what database, and what are the latency constraints?
answer: Google Docs estimation. Given: 50M DAU, average user edits 3 documents per day, average editing session 30 minutes, average document size 50KB, 5 concurrent editors per document on average for collaborative docs (only 10% of docs are collaborative). Step 1 — Operational Transform QPS: in a collaborative document, every keystroke is an operation. At 40 WPM typing speed with 5 characters per word, a user generates 40×5/60 ≈ 3.3 characters per second. With 50M DAU × 30% actively typing at any moment: 15M active typers × 3.3 chars/sec = 50M operations/second at peak. This is the heartbeat of the system — 50M tiny edit operations per second. Step 2 — Document storage: 50M users × 3 docs = 150M document versions per day. Each version delta (diff) ≈ 1KB. 150M × 1KB = 150GB/day of version deltas. Full document snapshots: 50M active documents × 50KB = 2.5TB. Step 3 — Latency requirement: collaborative editing requires < 100ms end-to-end latency for good user experience. This means: client sends operation → server receives and applies OT → server broadcasts to all collaborators → collaborators see change. All in under 100ms. This requires WebSocket connections (not HTTP polling), in-memory operation queuing (Redis), and operations broadcast via pub/sub. Step 4 — Architecture: each document has a server assigned to it (sticky routing). All editors of a document connect to the same server which applies Operational Transform to resolve conflicts.
hint: 50M ops/sec from keystrokes requires in-memory OT per document. Periodic DB persistence (every 5s) not per keystroke. All editors of one doc must hit same server.

question: Estimate the infrastructure for a global CDN serving static assets. Given 100TB of content, 1 billion daily asset requests, and a cache hit rate target of 95%, how many edge servers do you need and where do you place them?
answer: CDN infrastructure estimation — a complete worked example. Given: 100TB of total static content (images, CSS, JS, videos), 1 billion daily requests, target cache hit rate of 95%, average asset size of 500KB. Step 1 — Request QPS: 1B requests/day / 86,400 seconds ≈ 11,574 requests/second average. Peak: ~35,000 requests/second. Step 2 — Cache hit vs miss: 95% hit rate means 5% of requests are cache misses that go to origin. 35,000 × 5% = 1,750 requests/second to origin at peak — manageable for origin servers. Step 3 — Edge server capacity: each edge server with 10Gbps NIC serves: at 500KB average asset, 10Gbps / (500KB × 8) ≈ 2,500 requests/second per server. Peak 35,000 QPS / 2,500 per server = 14 edge servers globally (with 2× buffer: 28 servers). Step 4 — Edge cache storage: Pareto principle — top 20% of content gets 80% of requests. 100TB × 20% = 20TB of 'hot' content. Each edge server needs 20TB of SSD storage. With 28 edge servers × 20TB = 560TB total edge storage. Step 5 — Geographic placement: place edge servers in regions with highest user concentration. 8-10 regions covers 95% of global users: US East, US West, EU West, EU East, Asia Pacific (Tokyo), Southeast Asia (Singapore), South America (São Paulo), Middle East (Dubai).
hint: CDN estimation: 95% hit rate = 5% origin load (much lower). Edge servers sized by bandwidth not request count. Place in top traffic regions. Hot content = 20% of files, 80% of requests.
